summary: Gruber responds to Jean-Louis Gassée's argument that Apple should stop taking potshots at competitors now that it's the market leader. While semi-agreeing that Apple needs to be more graceful in its attacks, Gruber firmly disagrees that Apple should refrain from competitive marketing entirely. He draws a parallel to the Coke-Pepsi dynamic: the market leader doesn't name competitors directly but still fights back. He argues Apple's new 'There's iPhone, Then There's Everything Else' page follows this exact strategy by implying Samsung is inferior without naming it. The core argument is that being the champ doesn't mean being passive — it means fighting back with more finesse.
key_points: Apple's competitive marketing against Samsung isn't unprecedented — the 'Get a Mac' campaign was equally sharp-elbowed, just aimed at Microsoft/PCs
Market leaders must still fight back against competitors, but with greater grace and indirection — like Coke never naming Pepsi
Apple's strategy of framing it as 'iPhone vs. everything else' mirrors the Get a Mac approach of never naming specific competitors
Microsoft's stoic non-response to Apple's Get a Mac campaign actually hurt them, proving that ignoring competitive attacks is a losing strategy
Apple's real PR powerhouse is internal and dedicated, not an external firm as Gassée recommends
money_quotes: You can't market against your rival when you have no rival. Now Apple does, so now they are.
You don't start fights when you're the champ, but you do fight back when someone else starts one.
The last thing Apple should do is ignore Samsung, to just sit there and take it, stoically.
When you're the market leader, you do need to be more graceful, it's harder, but you still need to fight.
That's why Pepsi will trash Coke by name, but Coke will never mention Pepsi.
They're not tells. They're marketing. They're advertising. They're part and parcel of a brutal battle over a lucrative and important market.
One doesn't brag about one's performance in the boudoir; let happy partners sing your praise.
key_insight: Market leaders shouldn't start fights, but they must fight back with strategic indirection — naming no competitor while making clear that everything else is second-best.
